title: Very Noisy Option Prices and Inference Regarding the Volatility Risk Premium
abstract: The stylized fact that volatility is not priced in individual equity options does not withstand scrutiny. First, we show that the average return of heavily traded deep out-of-the-money call options on stocks is −116 basis points per day. Second, Fama-MacBeth estimates of the volatility risk premium in stock options are similar to those in S&P 500 Index call options. Third, the mean return of heavily traded delta-hedged at-the-money calls (puts) is −23 (−30) basis points. Fourth, the variance risk premium in stock options is negative. Our analysis highlights the importance of microstructure biases and robustness in empirical work with options.

article.introduction:
Determining whether volatility is pricedhas important consequences for interpreting implied volatilities and understanding the purpose of equity option markets. A volatility risk premium means that implied volatilities, whether they are derived from the Black-Scholes (1973) model or the model-free approach of Carr and Madan (2001) and Britten-Jones and Neuberger (2000), cannot be interpreted as unbiased forecasts of future realized volatility. Furthermore, knowing whether a volatility risk premium exists has important implications for understanding the purpose of options markets, in particular, the extent to which they can be seen as markets for volatility risk.
  Though understanding whether volatility is priced is a central issue in the option literature, the evidence is somewhat mixed. While both Coval and Shumway (2001) and Bakshi and Kapadia (2003a) find evidence consistent with volatility being negatively priced in index equity options, such evidence for individual stock options is weak or nonexistent (e.g., Bakshi and Kapadia (2003b), Carr and Wu (2009), and Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009)). This latter finding is puzzling because the volatilities of individual stocks are positively correlated with index volatility. Standard economic theory therefore implies that, to some extent, individual equity options are substitutes for options written on the index.
  We address this puzzle in this paper. Specifically, we examine four implications of a no-arbitrage stochastic volatility model to assess the volatility risk premium implied in equity and index options. The first is a nonmonotonic relation between the expected returns of calls and their moneyness, which is possible only in a stochastic volatility model when the volatility risk premium is negative. In the Black-Scholes model, the expected returns of call options on stocks are positive and increasing with moneyness. In contrast, in a stochastic volatility model, option expected returns are driven by both the expected return of the underlying asset and the volatility risk premium. The balance between these two effects may result in a nonmonotonic relation between expected call returns and their moneyness or even in negative expected returns for deep out-of-the-money (OTM) call options. We examine this implication using portfolios of call options sorted by their moneyness. Second, a negative volatility risk premium implies that the expected returns of delta-hedged options decrease with the sensitivity to underlying volatility (βσ$\beta _{\sigma }$). We examine this implication using Fama and MacBeth (1973, FM) regressions of delta-hedged option excess returns onβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$. Third, a negative volatility risk premium results in negative expected returns on delta-hedged calls and puts for all strikes and maturities. Fourth, a negative volatility risk premium implies that the average difference between the realized variance (RV$RV$) and the model-free implied variance (MFIV$MFIV$) derived from options prices is negative. This difference explicitly measures the variance risk premium (VRP$VRP$), which is a simple transformation of the volatility risk premium.
  Most importantly, we examine these standard implications of stochastic volatility models while systematically addressing the empirical challenges of working with options data. Individual equity option trading is highly concentrated, with 20% of all options accounting for about 90% of the market's total dollar volume. Actively traded options may have return patterns different from those of thinly traded options. In addition, option closing bid-ask spreads are large, at about 12% of the quote midpoint on average. This suggests that the estimation of option expected returns and risk premia may be strongly affected by biases due to measurement errors in prices, which are similar to those previously described in the asset pricing literature.1
  The empirical literature on options has typically attempted to address these empirical issues by focusing on options that are close to at-the-money (ATM) and discarding a large portion of the data (e.g., Jackwerth and Rubinstein (1996) and Bakshi and Kapadia (2003a)). The relative bid-ask spreads of deep OTM options are large, and the closing midquotes of deep in-the-money (ITM) options often violate no-arbitrage bounds. Hence, only using data on options that are close to ATM is a simple and sometimes effective method to deal with measurement errors in prices. This simplicity comes with potential costs, however, not only because it violates one of statistics' first principles—“Thou shall not throw data away” (Zhang, Mykland, and Aït-Sahalia (2005))—but also because it leads to conclusions that may not be robust across options of different moneyness. In addition, some research questions are particularly related to options that are far from ATM. For instance, theory indicates that the returns of deep OTM options are very sensitive to volatility risk. Therefore, the inclusion of deep OTM options in analysis of the volatility risk premium can be particularly informative.
  We adopt a comprehensive strategy to study the volatility risk premium that does not involve discarding data unnecessarily. First, we present both equal-weighted and dollar volume-weighted results to address the possibility that results may be driven by thinly traded options. Second, we address biases due to measurement errors in prices by adapting the bias-correction practices described in Blume and Stambaugh (1983), Fama (1984), Stambaugh (1988), and Asparouhova, Bessembinder, and Kalcheva (2010,2013) to the options market. Third, we test four different implications of a negative volatility risk premium using samples both with and without options that are far from ATM. We therefore gauge the extent to which our conclusions hold across different moneyness filters.
  We find compelling evidence that volatility is priced at the individual equity option level, particularly for options that are more heavily traded. First, we strongly reject the assumption that call option returns monotonically increase with option moneyness. In fact, contrary to the assumption that call option returns are driven only by the expected return of the underlying asset, we find that the bias-adjusted mean return of heavily traded deep OTM call options written on stocks in the S&P 500 is negative, at around −116 basis points (bps) per day. Second, our FM results show a negative volatility risk premium for high-volume call and put options. Third, we estimate that the excess mean return of heavily traded ATM delta-hedged calls (puts) is negative, at about −23 (−30) bps per day, while the mean excess return on heavily traded delta-hedged calls (puts) across all moneyness levels is also negative, at −35 (−29) bps per day. Fourth, we find a negative average variance risk premium (VRP$VRP$) of about −1.6 percentage points. Lastly, the evidence of a negative volatility risk premium, across all four of our methodologies, is stronger when we weight by trading volume. This result suggests that more heavily traded options may serve as better substitutes for index options.
  Our results emphasize the importance of systematically addressing a number of pitfalls associated with empirical options research. First, the sizeable effects of volume-weighting in our results demonstrates the importance of sensitivity analysis with respect to weighting when analyzing option returns. Second, our FM results show the importance of correcting for biases arising from measurement errors in prices, which are large enough to change the sign of the estimated volatility risk premium. These biases are especially important for ITM and OTM options, where they lead to substantial changes in the FM coefficient estimates on variables related to moneyness, such asβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$. Our FM results therefore establish the importance of using bias-adjustment methods, especially when working with options away from ATM. Third, ourVRP$VRP$results are qualitatively different if we focus on options that are closer to ATM. This result illustrates the importance of evaluating the robustness of conclusions to multiple sample selection criteria.
  We contribute to the literature by showing that volatility is, in fact, priced at the individual stock level once we address these empirical complications. The negative average returns of deep OTM call options that we document are consistent with those found by Ni (2008) using monthly call option returns. We extend Ni (2008) by attributing these negative average returns to a negative volatility risk premium. Bakshi and Kapadia (2003b) examine the delta-hedged returns of call options written on 25 stocks. We extend their analysis by examining the delta-hedged returns of both calls and puts on all of the stocks in the S&P 500 Index. Carr and Wu (2009) and Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009) estimate theVRP$VRP$.2We extend these studies by analyzing four different implications of priced volatility, instead of focusing only on theVRP$VRP$. Moreover, we show that our conclusions hold across different sample selection criteria.
  We also extend the literature on biases due to measurement errors in prices in the estimation of expected returns. We demonstrate that these biases are large in options that are far from ATM, and we show how they can be addressed. Thus, our bias-adjustment procedure makes analyses of data on deep OTM and ITM options possible, opening up the possibility of using data on these options instead of simply discarding them, as is common practice in the literature. We build on the procedure developed by Asparouhova, Bessembinder, and Kalcheva (2010,2013) to address some of the microstructure biases in average delta-hedged and unhedged option returns.3Our work is also related to Dennis and Mayhew (2009), who examine the effects of noisy option prices on the estimation of option pricing models, and Hentschel (2003), who studies the effects of noise on the estimation of the implied volatility smile. Our study differs from these papers by focusing on the effects of noisy prices on inferences about expected returns and risk premia. Moreover, two recent studies explore biases that are related to differences between the closing and intraday option prices. Eraker and Osterrieder (2022) show that the VIX is a biased estimate of implied volatility because the midpoint of the closing bid-ask prices is biased due to order flow pressure, while Goyenko and Zhang (2019) find biases in mean returns computed from closing prices that arise from differences between closing and intraday option prices. We differ from these studies because the biases we document are unrelated to any differences between intraday and closing prices. Rather, the biases that we explore depend only on the assumption that the prices (intraday or closing) used to compute returns are observed with zero-mean measurement errors.
  The remainder of the paper is as follows. SectionIdetails our data, while SectionIIdescribes our methodology. SectionIIIpresents the empirical results. SectionIVconcludes.
article.I. Data and Data Filters:
We use options written on stocks in the S&P 500 Index and the S&P 500 (SPX). Our sample period is from January 1996 to June 2019. Our primary data come from OptionMetrics' IvyDB data set, which contains closing bid-ask quotes on calls (C$C$) and puts (P$P$), closing stock prices (S$S$), stock returns, risk-free rates (r$r$), implied volatilities (σ$\sigma$), option deltas (Δ$\Delta$), vegas (ν$\nu$), and volume. TheΔ$\Delta$andν$\nu$of a given option in IvyDB are calculated with that option's own implied volatility, which depends on the closing midpoint of the option bid-ask quotes and on the closing price of the underlying asset. For index (stock) options,Δ$\Delta$andν$\nu$are calculated with the Black-Scholes (Cox, Ross, and Rubinstein) model. We compute option returns based on the midpoints of the option bid-ask quotes. As in Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009), we examine options with maturities from 10 to 44 trading days.4
  As is standard in the literature, we remove option prices that appear to be data errors. Removing these prices has the potential to induce look-ahead bias by filtering based on a price at timet$t$that would not be observed until after portfolio formation at timet−1$t-1$. We therefore remove these observations only as a last resort. Deleting them is necessary, however, because they are clearly erroneous and potentially influential. For instance, we eliminate an observation with a call ask price of $9,999 on a stock with a price of $40. These unreasonable observations, which are not used in any of our empirical analyses, comprise only about 0.3% of the sample. TheInternet Appendixcontains a detailed description of this procedure. In short, we eliminate erroneous or stub quotes, extremely large price reversals, and true arbitrage opportunities that account for bid-ask spreads in the option and the stock.
  We next impose baseline filters on the sample. It is common in the literature to impose filters based on relative bid-ask spreads, nonmissing implied volatilities, option midpoints, option open interest, and deltas.5We impose these filters in our baseline sample to analyze whether they create biases in the estimation of the volatility risk premium. The standard practice in the literature, which we follow in our baseline sample, is to impose these filters at the start of the holding period (t−1$t-1$), as doing so prevents look-ahead biases. However, this practice may result in sample selection (SS) biases when prices are observed with error. We discuss these biases in detail in SectionII.Bbelow.
  The summary statistics in TableIhighlight differences between options written on individual stocks and those written on the index. Specifically, the median implied volatility for call (put) options written on the index is about 14% (20%), which is substantially smaller than the median-implied volatility of options on individual stocks (31%). In addition, the mean bid-ask spreads for options written on individual stocks (Panels A and B) are noticeably larger than those for options written on the index (Panels C and D). Moreover, to the extent that measurement error variance is related to the width of bid-ask spreads, a comparison of stocks' effective spreads in Panel E with the option spreads in Panels A to D suggests that measurement error variance in option prices is likely much larger than that in stock prices.6
  Figure1plots the time series of the average closing bid-ask spread for calls and puts written on individual stocks and on the S&P 500 Index. The figure suggests that the biases we identify in this paper may still be strong at the end of our sample. Indeed, consistent with the findings of Battalio, Hatch, and Jennings (2004), the top panels show a substantial decrease in the closing bid-ask spreads of options on stocks in late 1999. However, spreads increased substantially in the 2013 to 2014 period, and by the end of the sample period, they are close to their values at the beginning of the sample.
  We define$VOL$\$VOL$as the dollar volume for a given option over the most recent five trading days, while$AVOL$\$AVOL$is the total (aggregated)$VOL$\$VOL$across options for a given stock. TablesIIandIIIshow time series averages of the daily medians of various option and stock characteristics sorted by$VOL$\$VOL$and$AVOL$\$AVOL$. TablesIIandIIIindicate that the distributions of$VOL$\$VOL$and$AVOL$\$AVOL$are highly skewed. Indeed, the%Total$VOL$\% \text{ Total }\$VOL$row in Panel A of TablesIIandIIIshows that the calls and puts in the top quintile of$VOL$\$VOL$account for 91% of the market's total$VOL$\$VOL$, while%Total$AVOL$\% \text{ Total }\$AVOL$in Panel B shows that the stocks in the top quintile$AVOL$\$AVOL$account for 89% of the market's total$AVOL$\$AVOL$of puts or calls. Panel A shows that options in the top quintile of$VOL$\$VOL$have relative spreads that are close to those for index options in Panel C. Panel A also shows that there is a strong positive relation between$VOL$\$VOL$and Median$AVOL$\$AVOL$. This result suggests that cross-sectional variation in$VOL$\$VOL$is driven largely by stock-level differences. Panel B of TablesIIandIIIconfirms that the stocks with a high$AVOL$\$AVOL$have options with lower bid-ask spreads, which are comparable to those of options on the S&P 500 Index. In addition, the stocks on the top 20% of$AVOL$\$AVOL$account for 50% of the total market cap in the S&P 500 Index, and their volatilities have high betas with respect to the VIX Index.
article.II. Empirical Methodology:
We start our analysis of the volatility risk premium in individual equity options by examining the mean returns of unhedged call options sorted by moneyness. Specifically, equation (1) reveals that the expected returns of call options do not necessarily increase monotonically with strike prices or moneyness whenλ∼σ<0$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }<0$. To see this, note that bothβ∼SC$\tilde{\beta }^C_{S}$andβ∼σC$\tilde{\beta }^C_{\sigma }$are positive and nonlinear functions of moneyness (see Figure2). Moreover, theβ∼σ$\tilde{\beta }_{\sigma }$of an ITM call option is close to zero, while theβ∼σ$\tilde{\beta }_{\sigma }$of an OTM call option is of the same order of magnitude as itsβ∼S$\tilde{\beta }_{S}$. That is, the expected return of ITM calls is driven largely by the expected return of the underlying asset, which is positive, while the expected return of OTM calls is driven by both the positive expected return of the underlying asset and the negative volatility risk premium whenλ∼σ<0$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }<0$. As a result,β∼σ,tfλ∼σ$\tilde{\beta }^f_{\sigma,t} \tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$in equation (1) is negative and may be the dominant term driving the expected returns of OTM call options, resulting in negative expected returns for deep OTM calls. In contrast,λ∼σ$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$is nonexistent in the Black-Scholes model, so that the expected return of a call option is driven only by its exposure to the underlying asset (β∼SC$\tilde{\beta }^C_S$), which increases monotonically with moneyness. We test whether the mean return of portfolios of call options sorted by moneyness increase monotonically with moneyness using the Wolak (1989) test.9
  Our second methodology is to use FM regressions to estimate the volatility risk premium in individual equity options. Equation (1) also indicates that FM regressions of delta-hedged excess returns on an option'sβ∼σ$\tilde{\beta }_{\sigma }$can be used to estimateλ∼σ$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$. Indeed, equation (1) implies that expected delta-hedged excess returns areEt−1[R∼f,t]−β∼S,t−1fEt−1[R∼S,t]≈β∼σ,t−1fλ∼σ$E_{t-1}[\tilde{R}_{f,t}] - \tilde{\beta }^f_{S,t-1} E_{t-1}[\tilde{R}_{S,t}] \approx \tilde{\beta }^f_{\sigma,t-1} \tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$. Hence, we can estimateλ∼σ$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$with FM regressions of delta-hedged option returns on option volatility betas.10The linear relation in equation (1) holds in continuous time, while the relation between options and underlying excess returns is nonlinear for discrete holding periods. To alleviate possible problems due to this nonlinearity, we estimate the FM regressions with high-frequency data (using daily instead of monthly returns). As in the case of the portfolios of unhedged call options sorted by moneyness, our FM regression analysis pools option contracts across stocks.
  A third methodology is to estimate the variance risk premium. The volatility risk premium implies a nonzero difference between the mean realized variance (RV¯$\overline{RV}$) and the mean model-free implied variance (MFIV¯$\overline{MFIV}$) (e.g., Carr and Wu (2009) and Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009)). The difference,RV¯−MFIV¯$\overline{RV}-\overline{MFIV}$, provides an estimate of the variance risk premium (VRP$VRP$) and is closely related to the volatility risk premium (λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$). Specifically, the instantaneousVRP$VRP$is by definition equal to the difference between the drifts of the variance process under the physical and the risk-neutral probability measures. An application of Ito's lemma implies that the instantaneousVRP$VRP$is2σtλσdt$2 \sigma _t \lambda _{\sigma } dt$.
  We computeVRPj$VRP_j$only for a secondary sample of underlying stocks for which accurate estimates of firm-levelVRPj$VRP_j$can be obtained. To do so, we follow Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009) and require a minimum number of options to computeMFIVj,t$MFIV_{j,t}$and a minimum number of time-series observations to compute the meansRV¯j$\overline{RV}_j$andMFIV¯j$\overline{MFIV}_j$. More specifically, to computeMFIVj,t$MFIV_{j,t}$we require at least three options, which must include at least one with strike price above and one with strike price below that of the ATM option.12All options must satisfy the filters in our baseline sample. In addition, a stock is included in the secondary sample if itsMFIVj,t$MFIV_{j,t}$can be computed for at least 36 months in the sample.
  Our fourth methodology examines the means of delta-hedged option returns. A final implication of equation (1) is that the mean excess returns of delta-hedged options on individual stocks are negative for all strikes and maturities when the volatility risk premium (λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$) is negative. We first test this implication using portfolios of options that are sorted byβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$, in which we pool option contracts across stocks. In addition, as in Bakshi and Kapadia (2003b), we also examine the mean daily returns of portfolios of delta-hedged options written on the same underlying stock. To obtain accurate estimates of the mean daily return of a portfolio of delta-hedged options on stockj$j$, we use only those stocks in our secondary sample of underlying stocks described above, and we require that the stock have at least three options (either calls or puts) in our baseline sample on dayt$t$.
  Naturally, the observed derivative and underlying asset prices contain measurement errors. The errors in prices affect observed returns. They also affect the observed optionβs$\beta{\rm s}$, because theβs$\beta{\rm s}$of an option are calculated with that option's own implied volatility, which depends, in turn, on the observed option and underlying asset prices. Following Blume and Stambaugh (1983), we assume that the observed price of a derivative isft=f∼t(1+εf,t)$f_{t}=\tilde{f}_{t}(1+\epsilon _{f,t})$, wheref∼t$\tilde{f}_{t}$represents the true price of the derivative andεf,t$\epsilon _{f,t}$is the zero-mean derivative price error term. We use an upper tilde (∼$\ \widetilde{}\ $) for variables without measurement errors, while variables affected by measurement errors in prices are unaccented.13
  The second is the regression coefficient (RC) bias, described by Asparouhova, Bessembinder, and Kalcheva (2010), which affects our FM regressions. To understand this bias, consider a simple model in which the true (unobserved) expected return of a derivative satisfies the univariate relation,Et−1[R∼f,i,t]=λ∼β∼i,t−1f$E_{t-1}[\tilde{R}_{f,i,t}]= \tilde{\lambda } \tilde{\beta }^f_{i,t-1}$. For simplicity, assume that we observeβ∼i,t−1f$\tilde{\beta }^f_{i,t-1}$and we estimate the regressionEt−1[Rf,i,t]=λ0+λβ∼i,tf$E_{t-1}[R_{f,i,t}]=\lambda _0 + \lambda \tilde{\beta }^f_{i,t}$. Under some simplifying assumptions, the ordinary least squares (OLS) estimate ofλ$\lambda$is approximatelyλ∼(1+E[εf,t−12])$\tilde{\lambda } (1 +E[\epsilon _{f,t-1}^2])$.15Intuitively, the RC bias is a generalization of the MR bias to an FM regression setting. To see this, note that the MR bias,E[f∼t/f∼t−1]E[εf,t−12]$E[\tilde{f}_{t}/\tilde{f}_{t-1}] E[\epsilon _{f,t-1}^2]$, increases with the expected return of the derivative. Hence, the MR bias changes withβ∼i,t−1f$\tilde{\beta }^f_{i,t-1}$in our simple model for expected return andλ$\lambda$is a biased estimate ofλ∼$\tilde{\lambda }$. Asparouhova, Bessembinder, and Kalcheva (2010) describe the RC bias in more generality and show that a nonzero correlation betweenE[εf,i,t−12]$E[\epsilon _{f,i,t-1}^2]$andβ∼i,t−1f$\tilde{\beta }^f_{i,t-1}$will produce additional bias.
  The third is the SS bias, which potentially affects the estimation of both mean returns and FM regressions. To understand this bias, assume that our intent is to estimate the population expected return of a certain type of derivative (e.g., the unconditional expected return of call options,E[RC,i,t]$E[R_{C,i,t}]$) with a sample that is constructed with some data filters (e.g., price at timet−1$t-1$is above $0.10 or implied volatility at timet−1$t-1$is not missing). Mathematically, the expected return of a derivative in our sample isE[Rf,i,t|1Sample(fi,t−1)=1]$E[R_{f,i,t} | \mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})=1]$, where1Sample(fi,t−1)$\mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})$is a function indicating whether the derivativei$i$is within our sample at timet−1$t-1$. The measurement error infi,t−1$f_{i,t-1}$affects bothRf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$and the indicator function1Sample(fi,t−1)$\mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})$, inducing a spurious covariance betweenRf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$and1Sample(fi,t−1)$\mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})$that, in turn, results in a bias. Specifically,E[Rf,i,t|1Sample(fi,t−1)]−E[Rf,i,t]=Cov[Rf,i,t,1Sample(fi,t−1)]/E[1Sample(fi,t−1)]$E[R_{f,i,t} | \mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})] - E[R_{f,i,t}] = Cov[R_{f,i,t},\mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})]/E[\mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})]$.
  An example of how the SS bias arises in our context is the common practice of discarding options without implied volatility at timet−1$t-1$. To see how this bias works in this case, note that negative values ofεf,i,t−1$\epsilon _{f,i,t-1}$lead to low values of observed option prices (fi,t−1$f_{i,t-1}$) that tend to be reversed, resulting in high values ofRf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$. These negative measurement errors may also result in observed midpoint option prices (fi,t−1$f_{i,t-1}$) that are below lower arbitrage bounds and hence do not have a computed implied volatility. As a result, deleting these observations from the sample results inCov[Rf,i,t,1Sample(fi,t−1)]<0$\textit{Cov}[R_{f,i,t},\mathbb {1}_{Sample}(f_{i,t-1})]<0$, leading to a negative bias in the estimated expected return.
  Finally, the fourth bias is the correlated error in variables (CEIVs) bias, which also potentially affects both portfolio sorts and FM regressions. In the FM regression context, the CEIV bias renders the estimatedλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$in equation (3) spurious becauseβσ,i,t−1f$\beta ^f_{\sigma,i,t-1}$and returns (Rf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$andRS,t$R_{S,t}$) are infected by the same measurement errors in option and underlying prices at timet−1$t-1$. In portfolio sorts, the estimated expected return isE[Rf,i,t|1Portfolio(fi,t−1)]$E[R_{f,i,t} | \mathbb {1}_{Portfolio}(f_{i,t-1})]$, where1Portfolio(fi,t−1)$\mathbb {1}_{Portfolio}(f_{i,t-1})$is a function indicating whether the derivative is within the portfolio of derivatives satisfying the target characteristic at timet−1$t-1$. The measurement error infi,t−1$f_{i,t-1}$affects bothRf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$and the indicator function1Portfolio(fi,t−1)$\mathbb {1}_{Portfolio}(f_{i,t-1})$, inducing a spurious covariance betweenRf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$and1Portfolio(fi,t−1)$\mathbb {1}_{Portfolio}(f_{i,t-1})$that leads, in turn, to a bias.
  In addition to addressing these four estimation biases, we provide several examples of how the conclusions of empirical option studies may not be robust to the sample selection criteria. In particular, TablesIIandIIIshow that the majority of options in our sample are thinly traded, with only 20% of the options accounting for about 90% of the volume traded. Equal weighting therefore amounts to giving tremendous influence to a large number of contracts that are of little economic significance, a point similar to that made by Hou, Xue, and Zhang (2020) in an analysis of equity anomalies. Moreover, we examine the effects of including deep OTM options in our sample. These are highly sensitive to the price of volatility risk but are often discarded in empirical studies (e.g., Jackwerth and Rubinstein (1996)). Our empirical work examines whether our results are robust to these changes in weighting and sample selection.
  We address the MR, RC, CEIV, and SS biases with a bias-adjustment procedure based on best practices from the asset pricing literature. Our approach relies on the assumption that price errors are independent in the time series and independent across stocks, calls, and puts. In addition, the price errors are assumed to be independent of true prices. TableIVsummarizes our bias-adjustment and robustness procedures, which we describe below.
  MR bias. Equal-weighted observed mean returns are biased estimates of the actual unobserved expected return. The MR bias is approximately equal toE[f∼t/f∼t−1]E[εf,t−12]$E[\tilde{f}_{t}/\tilde{f}_{t-1}] E[\epsilon _{f,t-1}^2]$
  Estimate expected returns with the weighted average—∑i=1Nwf,i,t−1Rf,i,t/∑j=1Nwf,i,t−1$\sum _{i=1}^{N} w_{f,i,t-1} R_{f,i,t}/\sum _{j=1}^{N} w_{f,i,t-1}$—wherewi,t−1$ w_{i,t-1}$is the gross return of the derivativei$i$at timet−1$t-1$(fi,t−1/fi,t−2$f_{i,t-1}/f_{i,t-2}$). The prices (fi,t−1${f}_{i,t-1}$) with large errors have larger weights and smaller returns, resulting in estimates that are approximately unbiased.
  RC bias. Measurement errors in prices lead to biased OLS-estimated coefficients of FM regressions because the MR bias (E[f∼t/f∼t−1]E[εf,t−12]$E[ {\tilde{f}}_{t}/{\tilde{f}}_{t-1}]E\operatorname{[}{\varepsilon}_{f,t-1}^{2}]$) increases with the expected return of the derivative, making the OLS estimate ofλ∼σ$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$biased.
  
  
  Estimate FM regressions with WLS using lagged gross returns(fi,t−1/fi,t−2)$({f}_{i,t-1}/{f}_{i,t-2})$as the weights. As in the MR bias adjustment, the prices (fi,t−1${f}_{i,t-1}$) with large measurement errors have larger weights and smaller returns, resulting in approximately unbiased WLS estimates ofλ∼σ$\tilde{\lambda }_{\sigma }$.
  CEIV bias. Affects portfolio sorts when the same measurement errors affect the sorting variable and the option return. The same errors affect the observedRf,i,t$R_{f,i,t}$andβσ,i,t−1f$\beta ^f_{\sigma,i,t-1}$in the regression:
  
  
  Sort options byt−2$t-2$instead oft−1$t-1$variables. Break the spurious relation between dependent and independent variables withβσ,if$\beta ^f_{\sigma,i}$measured int−2$t-2$:
  
  
  SS bias. The same measurement errors in prices affect both returns and the sample selection. An example is discarding options without implied volatility at timet−1$t-1$. The SS bias affects the estimation of both mean returns and FM regressions.
  Break the spurious relation between returns and sample selection with sample selection criteria based ont−2$t-2$variables. Use the option's delta at timet−2$t-2$to computeβS,i,t−1f${\beta}_{S,i,t-1}^{f}$asSt−1/ft−1×ΔS,i,t−2f${S}_{t-1}/{f}_{t-1}\ensuremath{\times{}}{\mathrm{\Delta}}_{S,i,t-2}^{f}$when the implied volatility at timet−1$t-1$is missing.
  Robustness. Empirical results may be driven by the majority of options that are thinly traded. Sample selection criteria are often based on moneyness. Hence, conclusions may be only applicable to a subset of options.
  Report both equal-weighted and volume weighted results. Report results based on samples that included and exclude deep OTM options.
  Following Asparouhova, Bessembinder, and Kalcheva (2010,2013), we address the MR and RC biases by weighting observations proportionally by their lagged gross returns. That is, derivativei$i$is weighted bywi,t−1=fi,t−1/fi,t−2$w_{i,t-1} = f_{i,t-1}/f_{i,t-2}$when calculating mean returns or estimating the FM regression in equation (5) with weighted least squares (WLS).16
  We dispense with SS bias by adjusting our sample selection to break its dependency on the option prices used to estimate returns. Specifically, we use anadjustedoption sample that is built with the same type of filters as the baseline filters described in SectionI. However, these filters are imposed ont−2$t-2$prices in our adjusted sample instead oft−1$t-1$prices, as in our baseline sample. Moreover, when the option's delta at timet−1$t-1$is missing, which occurs when implied volatility cannot be calculated, we use the option's delta at timet−2$t-2$to computeβS,i,t−1f$\beta ^f_{S,i,t-1}$in equation (5) asΔS,i,t−2f×St−1/ft−1$\Delta ^f_{S,i,t-2} \times S_{t-1}/f_{t-1}$. This procedure addresses the deletion of observations without implied volatility at timet−1$t-1$from the sample. Note that this procedure does not imply that we are using prices that allow for arbitrage. This is because OptionMetrics computes implied volatilities based on closing stock prices and option midquotes, which results in missing implied volatilities when these prices do not satisfy no-arbitrage bounds. However, an actual arbitrage opportunity requires that the option's ask price, rather than the midquote, be below the no-arbitrage lower bound. Furthermore, the no-arbitrage bound depends on the stock's end-of-day bid or ask price instead of the stock closing price, which is almost always outside the bid-ask spread (Bogousslavsky and Muravyev (2023)).
  We use two procedures to examine the robustness of our conclusions to the sample selection criteria. First, to address the possibility that our conclusions are driven by the majority of thinly traded options, we also present results weighted by option-level dollar volume ($VOL$\$VOL$) when taking averages at the option level or by stock-level option volume ($AVOL$\$AVOL$) when taking averages at the stock level. Specifically, our bias-adjusted volume-weighted procedure weights by$VOLt−2×fi,t−1/fi,t−2$\$VOL_{t-2} \times f_{i,t-1}/f_{i,t-2}$. Asparouhova, Bessembinder, and Kalcheva (2010) show that bias adjustment only requires weights that are proportional to thet−1$t-1$price. Gross return weighting is a special case that approximates equal weighting. We use$VOL$\$VOL$(or$AVOL$\$AVOL$) at timet−2$t-2$instead oft−1$t-1$to preserve proportionality, since$VOLt−1$\$VOL_{t-1}$also depends on thet−1$t-1$option price.17Note that weighting by$VOL$\$VOL$or$AVOL$\$AVOL$is different from simply discarding options that are thinly traded because wealsoreport results that are not weighted by volume, which allows us to examine whether our conclusions are mainly due to the thinly traded options in the sample.
  Second, to address the possibility that our moneyness selection criteria drive our results, we also report results based on a sample without deep OTM options. Specifically, we closely follow Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009) by using call options withΔC>0.15$\Delta ^C>0.15$and put options withΔP<−0.05$\Delta ^P<-0.05$. To facilitate comparability of our results with those of Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009) and across all of our methodologies, our subsample without deep OTM options only includes options on underlying stocks for which accurate estimates of firm-levelVRPj$VRP_j$can be obtained. SectionII.Aabove describes the criteria used to build this secondary sample.
article.III. Results:
We start our empirical work by testing whether the expected return of call options increases monotonically with moneyness. For this purpose, TableVreports statistics of portfolios of call options written on stocks sorted by moneyness. The results reveal that the medianβS$\beta _S$of deep ITM call options is 4.97, which is about 33 times the medianβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$(0.15). In contrast, the medianβS$\beta _{S}$of deep OTM call options is 22.01, which is only about 1.5 times the medianβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$(13.17).18Therefore, these factor sensitivities, together with equation (1), indicate that the expected returns of deep ITM calls are driven mostly by the expected returns on the underlying asset, while the expected returns of deep OTM calls are driven by both the expected returns on the underlying asset and the volatility risk premium. Hence, in contrast to the Black-Scholes model, ifλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$is negative, the expected returns of calls do not necessarily increase monotonically with their moneyness.19
  Consistent with volatility being negatively priced in individual stock options, the results in TableVreveal that the average return of calls does not increase monotonically with moneyness. The right column of the table displays thep-value of a Wolak (1989) test. The null hypothesis in this test is that average call returns increase monotonically with moneyness. Thep-values indicate that this null hypothesis is rejected at the usual significance levels.
  The results in TableValso suggest that volatility pricing is stronger among more heavily traded stock options, which may serve as better substitutes for index options. Indeed, the bias-adjusted$VOL$\$VOL$-weighted average return of deep OTM options is negative, at about −116 bps per day. Without weighting by$VOL$\$VOL$, deep OTM calls have a positive but insignificant mean return of about 39 bps per day. Although this mean return is not negative, the Wolakp-value is 0.03, indicating that the null hypothesis that the mean return of calls increases with moneyness is still rejected at the 5% level. High$VOL$\$VOL$options have low bid-ask spreads and are written on stocks with a high volume of traded options, large market capitalization, and a highβΔVIX$\beta _{\Delta VIX}$(TablesIIandIII). Hence, heavily traded options are more attractive to investors who would otherwise use index options.
  The evidence in TableVdepends, to some extent, on including deep OTM options in our sample. The options in the high-moneyness portfolio of individual equity (index) options have a medianΔ$\Delta$of 0.08 (0.05). Nevertheless, when we filter out deep OTM call options, we continue to find a nonmonotonic increasing relation between average individual equity call option returns and moneyness when weighting by$VOLi,t−2$\$VOL_{i,t-2}$. Indeed, the results in TableVbased on the subsample without deep OTM call options show a Wolakp-value of 0.04 when weighting by$VOLi,t−2$\$VOL_{i,t-2}$. Note that the portfolio with high-moneyness is absent in this case because this subsample does not include call options withΔC<0.15$\Delta ^C<0.15$. As in the case of individual stock calls, the results in TableVIreveal that the average returns of calls on the S&P 500 Index also vary nonmonotonically with moneyness. The Black-Scholes model cannot explain this pattern.
  While the results in TablesVandVIare all bias-adjusted, TableVIIgives a first look at the biases in mean option returns. In theInternet Appendix, we show with simulations that our bias adjustment methodology performs well. We may therefore interpret the difference between bias-unadjusted and bias-adjusted means as measures of estimation biases. TableVIIpresents these differences.20
  The results in TableVII, Panel A, show that estimation biases are especially important for both low-moneyness (deep ITM and ITM) and high-moneyness (deep OTM) calls.21Biases sometimes have different signs and partially offset one other, resulting in a total bias that often changes sign across moneyness. Specifically, the SS+CEIV bias is quite large for both deep ITM and moderately ITM options, at about −13 bps and −25 bps per day, respectively. These biases are also very large for OTM and deep OTM options, at about −23 bps and −133 bps. The MR bias is largest for deep OTM options at about 79 bps. The total bias is largest, in absolute value, for the deep ITM (−12 bps), moderately ITM (−22 bps), and deep OTM (−53 pbs) portfolios. These numbers are the same order of magnitude as the adjusted estimates themselves, which emphasizes their economic significance.
  TableVII, Panel B, indicates that the MR bias is also quite large for OTM options on the index, even though the total bias in index options is small. The results for the deep OTM portfolio show an MR bias of about 66 bps per day, but the total bias is just −7 bps as a result of the negative and highly economically significant SS+CEIV bias. In general, however, these two biases do not offset one another. In Portfolio 4, for example, the SS+CEIV bias is positive and economically large, at about 22 bps. It therefore reinforces the positive MR bias, leading to a total bias of 32 bps. The magnitude of this value indicates that bias adjustment is critical even for index options.
  The bias estimates in TableVIIare large, but they are nevertheless consistent with economic theory. For instance, Blume and Stambaugh (1983) show that the MR bias is approximatelyE[εf,t−12]$E[\epsilon _{f,t-1}^2]$. The variance of the measurement errors is not directly observed in the data, but Roll (1984) shows that, after multiplication by −1, the autocovariance in daily returns provides an estimate ofE[εf,t−12]$E[\epsilon _{f,t-1}^2]$and therefore of the size of the MR bias. In the case of individual equity options, this autocovariance is about 93 bps (1.41 bps) for deep OTM (deep ITM) calls. In the case of index options, this autocovariance is about 52 bps (0.02 bps) for deep OTM (deep ITM) calls.22Consistent with the theory, these autocovariances are of the same magnitude as the MR biases estimated in Panels A and B of TableVII.
  The nonmonotonicity of the SS+CEIV bias in Panel A is the result of the sign of the CEIV bias changing from one portfolio to the next. To understand why this occurs, consider the case of an ITM call option whose strike price and implied volatility place it close to the cutoff between the portfolio with low moneyness and Portfolio 2. If this option has a positive price error, its observed implied volatility increases, making it more likely to be assigned to Portfolio 2. (Because the strike price will be a smaller number of standard deviations from the spot price.) The positive price error also means that the subsequent observed return is likely to be low, resulting in a negative bias in Portfolio 2's average return. If, instead, the error were negative, then the observed moneyness would be reduced. The option would then be more likely to be placed in the low-moneyness portfolio, where its negative price error would lead to a high subsequent observed return and positive CEIV bias. The simulations in theInternet Appendixconfirm this pattern in the CEIV bias.
  In some cases, the SS+CEIV bias for index options has a different sign than that for individual equity options. For example, Panel A shows that the SS+CEIV bias on deep ITM individual equity calls is negative, at about −13 bps, while Panel B shows that the corresponding value for index calls is positive, at about 1 bp. This difference stems from the fact that many ITMstockoptions have midpoint prices in violation of an arbitrage bound. In fact, about 20.08% of the deep ITM options on stocks have missing implied volatility, while this is true for deep ITMindexoptions for only 1.91% of the sample. Following common practice in the literature, our unadjusted sample does not include ITM options whose midquotes do not satisfy arbitrage conditions.23Not including these options results in an economically large and negative SS bias. Such a large negative SS bias reveals an important issue with the standard practice of excluding from empirical work stock options for which the midpoint price is in violation of an arbitrage bound.
  The economically significant SS+CEIV biases for deep OTM stock and index options can also be understood as a result of negative autocovariance. To make sense of the SS bias for the deep OTM calls, note that the sample selection criteria tend to exclude options with negative price errors. Among the options that we select using our adjusted method, which imposes filters at timet−2$t-2$, about 30% would be excluded if the same sample selection filters were also imposed at timet−1$t-1$. On average, these options had a −20% return on dayt−1$t-1$, but strong negative autocovariance results in their having an average return of 4% on dayt$t$. Excluding these options from the sample therefore decreases the expected portfolio return by about 1.2% (30%×$\times$4%) per day.
  The consistency between the biases in TableVIIand the negative autocovariance in returns described above suggests that the biases we document are, in fact, the result of errors in prices. A skeptical reader may nevertheless view the large differences in the mean returns of the high-moneyness portfolio in TableVIIas evidence that deep OTM options should be avoided. While this practice may be acceptable in some cases, the simulation analysis in theInternet Appendixshows that our bias adjustment approach is reliable. Therefore, our bias adjustment procedure opens up the possibility of using data on deep OTM options instead of simply discarding them. This is especially important for addressing research questions for which options that are far from ATM are useful (e.g., the price of volatility risk) or even critical (e.g., questions related to tail risk).
  We next examine the sign of the volatility risk premium (λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$) in FM regressions. The estimatedλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$represents the expected excess daily return on a portfolio with unit exposure to volatility risk (βσ=1$\beta _{\sigma }=1$). TableVIII, Panel A, shows the results of the bias-adjusted FM regressions in equation (5).
  For call options on individual stocks, we again find different results depending on whether we weight by$VOL$\$VOL$, suggesting that the most heavily traded call options on stocks have a negative volatility risk premium while thinly traded call options do not. When we do not weight by$VOL$\$VOL$, the bias-adjusted estimate ofλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$for call options is negative, at −0.32 bps, but insignificant. In contrast, the bias-adjusted FM regression using call options and$VOL$\$VOL$weights results in an estimatedλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$of −10.42 bps. That is, volatility pricing is stronger among heavily traded options written on stocks with a high volume of traded options, which tended to be on larger stocks that have a higherβΔVIX$\beta _{\Delta VIX}$(TablesIIandIII). As a result, the price of volatility in options on large market capitalization stocks appears to inherit the price of volatility in index options.
  The coefficients in Panel A indicate that volatility is negatively priced in put options written on stocks after adjusting for biases.24The bias-adjusted estimates ofλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$for these options are between −3.65 bps and −5.04 bps.
  The results in TableVIII, Panel A, are not driven by deep OTM options. The right columns of TableVIIIshow the results for the subsample of options that excludes deep OTM puts and calls. The point estimates ofλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$are slightly closer to zero when excluding deep OTM options, but they are still mostly negative and significant. Specifically, for call options without$VOL$\$VOL$weights,λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$is positive (1.16), though not significant. In contrast, for call options with$VOL$\$VOL$weights,λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$is −7.55 bps and statistically different from zero. For put options,λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$is always negative and significant.
  Overall, the bias-adjustedλσs$\lambda _{\sigma }{\rm s}$in Panel A are inconsistent with prior work arguing that volatility is unpriced in individual stock options. Importantly, however, our different conclusion only holds as the result of bias adjustment. In Panel B of TableVIII, which shows the results of FM regressions without bias adjustment, point estimates ofλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$are mostly positive for individual equity options.
  The results in TableIXindicate that volatility is also negatively priced in options written on the S&P 500 Index. The estimated volatility risk premium for index puts is about −15 bps, which is economically larger than that estimated with index call options (between −4.91 bps and −6.36 bps).25In general, the volatility risk premium in individual equity put options is about the same as it is for S&P 500 Index call options, with estimates around −5 bps.
  In contrast to individual equity options, the effects of bias adjustment in the FM regressions are not as striking for index options. Index puts, for example, give an estimatedλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$of around −15 bps under bias adjustment, but without bias adjustment, they are close, at about −13 bps. Volume weighting has little impact, and biases in call-based estimates are proportionally similar. Of course, this does not imply that biases are always small for index options, as TableVIIalready provides a counterexample.
  As shown in equation (1), a negative volatility risk premium implies that excess delta-hedged option returns should be negative, on average, for all strikes and maturities. In this section, we test this implication by first examining the average excess returns ofβσ$\beta _\sigma$-sorted portfolios of delta-hedged options on stocks and on the S&P 500 Index. We then analyze stock-level portfolios of delta-hedged options.
  TableX, which reports results forβσ$\beta _\sigma$-sorted portfolios of options written on individual stocks, shows significant evidence of a negative volatility risk premium for individual equity options. The results in Panel B for Portfolio 3 show that the excess mean return of heavily traded ATM delta-hedged calls (puts) is negative and significant, at about −23 (−30) bps per day. The results are stronger for puts. Specifically, the bias-adjusted mean returns of delta-hedged puts in Panel B are statistically different from zero for ITM (lowβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$), ATM, and OTM (highβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$) portfolios, whether or not we weight by$VOL$\$VOL$. Similarly to TablesVandVIII, calls written on large capitalization stocks with heavily traded options have a significant negative volatility risk premium, while thinly traded call options do not. Indeed, for calls on individual stocks, the mean delta-hedged returns in Panel B are negative but are significantly different from zero only for deep ITM calls. When weighting by volume, however, the mean returns of delta-hedged calls are negative and statistically significant.
  TableXalso clarifies the sources of the large biases in the FM regressions with individual equity options that were shown in Panel B of TableVIII. Specifically, TableXindicates that biases in FM regressions derive from biases in the mean returns of both ITM and OTM options, which can be estimated by comparing the mean returns in Panels C and B. For individual equity options, the table shows that the bias on the equal-weighted mean return of ITM options (lowβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$) is negative, at about −30 bps for both puts and calls.26In contrast, the bias on the equal-weighted mean return of OTM options (highβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$) is positive, at around 11 bps for calls and 45 bps for puts. The combination of the ITM and OTM biases leads to a sign change in the H-L column that is consistent with the sizeable effects of bias adjustment in theλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$coefficients in the FM regressions.
  TableXIshows significant evidence of a negative volatility risk premium for index options. The magnitudes of the mean returns on index options are generally greater than those on individual equity options. This is consistent with the fact that the average values ofβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$, as shown in Panel A of TablesXandXI, are smaller for options on stocks than they are for the index. In particular, Figure2shows that theβσs$\beta _{\sigma }{\rm s}$of options written on the index are about double those of the options written on individual stocks. The difference inβσs$\beta _{\sigma }{\rm s}$arises as a result of the average-index-implied volatility being around half that of options on individual stocks, given thatβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$decreases with volatility. These differences inβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$explain why the mean delta-hedged returns of index options are economically more significant, even though the volatility risk premia (λσs$\lambda _{\sigma }{\rm s}$) estimates in TablesVIIIandIXare more similar.
  TableXIalso reveals why the FM regressions with index options in Panel B of TableIXhave small biases. For index options, biases for deep OTM options in TableXIare similar to those for stocks in TableX.27However, the bias in ITM (lowβσ$\beta _{\sigma }$) options is positive for index options.28This is consistent with the results in TableVIIand can be attributed to a small fraction of deep ITM index options with missing implied volatilities. The result is a smaller bias in H-L means, relative to individual equity options, which explains why bias adjustment is not as consequential in FM regressions estimated using index options.
  While the analyses in TablesVtoXaggregate options across stocks, TableXIIexamines separate portfolios for each stock. Using all call or put options that pass our filters and are written on the subset of stocks for which we have sufficient data, as described in SectionII.A, we form stock-level portfolios of delta-hedged option positions. Panel A shows the results of tests of the hypothesis that the returns of portfolios with delta-hedged options (Rj,t$R_{j,t}$) on the same stockj$j$have means equal to zero. Consistent with Bakshi and Kapadia (2003b), we find weak evidence of volatility risk premia based on stock-level portfolios of delta-hedged call options. Indeed, Panel A shows that only 58 out of 692 stocks have statistically significant negative delta-hedged call mean returns at the 5% level.29Bias adjustment does not make much of a difference for these results. However, the delta-hedged returns on put options show a different picture, with 252 stocks that have statistically significant negative mean returns.
  The results in Panels A and B of TableXIIreinforce Carr and Wu's (2009) conclusion that stock-level analysis is too noisy for reliable inference. Panel B therefore aggregates across stocks, which increases statistical power by substantially decreasing the amount of noise in returns. Specifically, Panel B shows the time-series average of both the equal-weighted and the$AVOLj,t−2$\$AVOL_{j,t-2}$-weighted averages ofRj,t$R_{j,t}$across stocks. Consistent with the results in TablesVtoX, Panel B indicates that volatility is negatively priced in individual equity options, especially among stocks that have a higher volume of option trading ($AVOLj$\$AVOL_{j}$). Indeed, the mean excess return on delta-hedged calls (puts) weighted by$AVOLj$\$AVOL_{j}$is negative, at −35 (−29) bps per day. Panel C shows analogous results consistent with volatility being priced in options on the S&P 500 Index.
  TableXIIalso shows the results for the subsample without deep OTM options. These results confirm that our conclusion that volatility is priced in individual-equity options does not depend on the inclusion of deep OTM options in our baseline sample, although mean delta-hedged returns are in general closer to zero when excluding those options. For instance, Panel B shows that the bias-adjusted mean return of delta-hedged puts in the sample withΔP<−0.05$\Delta _P<-0.05$is −17.96 bps, while it is −19.34 bps in the sample including deep OTM puts. However, these small differences do not warrant a change in the interpretation that volatility is priced in individual equity options. Moreover, the bias adjustment procedure does not qualitatively affect the results in TableXII.
  TableXIIIdisplays the results of theVRP$VRP$estimation. The results in the first column of TableXIIIindicate that volatility is priced in individual equity options and especially in options written on large-market-capitalization stocks with heavily traded options. The first column of TableXIIIexamines the average difference betweenRV$RV$andMFIV$MFIV$, estimating theMFIV$MFIV$in our secondary sample of underlying stocks. The results indicate that volatility is negatively priced in individual equity options. Indeed, Panel A shows that 334 out of 692 (109+583) stocks have aVRP$VRP$that is negative and statistically significant at the 5% level. In addition, Panel B shows that the equal averageVRP$VRP$(RV¯−MFIV¯$\overline{RV} - \overline{MFIV}$) across stocks is about −1.6% and that the$AVOL$\$AVOL$-weighted averageVRP$VRP$is about −2.7%. Interestingly, the negativeVRPs$VRP{\rm s}$in TableXIIIare approximately consistent with the negative values ofλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$estimated in TableVIII. Recall that the instantaneousVRP$VRP$is2σtλσdt$2\sigma _t\lambda _{\sigma } dt$. Replacingσt$\sigma _t$with 35% (the average volatility of stocks from TableI),λσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$with −5 bps per day (approximately the averageλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$of Panel A of TableVIII), anddt$dt$with 30 days, we arrive at a monthlyVRP$VRP$of −1 percentage point, which is reasonably close to the averageVRP$VRP$in column (1) of TableXIII, Panel A.
  The results in the second column of TableXIIIindicate thatVRP$VRP$estimation at the individual equity level is not robust to the exclusion of deep OTM options. To exclude these options,Kmin,j,t$K_{\text{min},j,t}$in equation (2) is set as the smallest observed strike price for whichΔj,tP(K)<−0.05$\Delta ^P_{j,t}(K)<-0.05$, andKmax,j,t$K_{\text{max},j,t}$is the largest observed strike price for whichΔj,tC(K)>0.15$\Delta ^C_{j,t}(K)>0.15$. In addition, theMFIV$MFIV$in column (2) is computed using the methodology that is most common in the literature, which uses only OTM options (e.g., Carr and Wu (2009) and Driessen, Maenhout, and Vilkov (2009)). That is, the sample used in the second column of TableXIIIdiffers from the first approach in that it does not use options that are deeper OTM and does not use an ITM call (put) to infer the implied volatility of an unavailable OTM put (call) with the same strike.30In contrast to the methodology in the first column, the results excluding deep OTM options suggest that volatility is not priced on options written on individual stocks. Indeed,RV¯−MFIV¯$\overline{RV} - \overline{MFIV}$is not statistically different from zero in the second column of Panel B.
  An alternative but inconsistent explanation for the findings in TableXIIIis that the results in column (1) are due to unreliable deep OTM option prices. Under this interpretation, deep OTM options should be filtered out of the sample. The issue with this view is that it is in conflict with the results in TablesVtoXII, which indicate that volatility is priced even in the sample without deep OTM options. We are therefore left with the explanation that theVRP$VRP$estimation is not robust to excluding deep OTM options from the sample.
  To clarify why theVRP$VRP$estimation is not robust to the sample selection criteria, Panel A of Figure3displays the average of the implied volatilities of the OTM options used to numerically calculate the integral in equation (2) as a function of the strike-to-spot ratio.31The continuous line in the figure shows that restricting the sample to OTM call (put) options withΔC>0.15$\Delta ^C>0.15$(ΔP<−0.05$\Delta ^P<-0.05$) results in the use of options with implied volatilities that are on average lower than those observed in the sample with deep OTM options, which, in turn, leads to a smaller average integrand in Panel B, a smallerMFIV$MFIV$, and aVRP$VRP$closer to zero. In contrast, the sample with deep OTM options includes a larger range of strike prices, which results in largerMFIV$MFIV$and aVRP$VRP$consistent with the values ofλσ$\lambda _{\sigma }$estimated with the bias-adjusted FM regressions in TableVIII. That is, Figure3indicates that theVRP$VRP$estimation is not robust to the inclusion of deep OTM options in the sample because it relies on the extrapolation of implied volatilities to infer the actual volatility of the options that are excluded from the sample. In contrast, the methodologies in TablesVtoXIIdo not rely on this extrapolation.32
article.IV. Conclusion:
The stylized fact that volatility is not priced in individual equity options does not withstand scrutiny. We find that volatility is strongly priced in individual equity puts and in heavily traded calls. We also find that the volatility risk premium estimated in individual equity options is about the same as that estimated with index call options. These conclusions are not driven by deep OTM options, but rather depend to some extent on empirical procedures that adjust for microstructure biases and on pooling options across stocks instead of analyzing the volatility risk premium on a stock-level basis.
  Our work demonstrates how microstructure biases, sample selection, and portfolio weighting can reverse conclusions from empirical analysis of options. The empirical options literature applies many different approaches to examine a variety of research questions. While we focus on the specific problem of estimating volatility risk premia, our findings suggest that it is important to explicitly address issues related to biases and robustness in a wide range of analyses of options data.
  It is reasonable to interpret the larger and more significant volatility risk premium in higher volume options on individual stocks as evidence that these options are better substitutes for index options. After all, these options tend to have lower bid-ask spreads and are written on larger firms with greater sensitivity to market volatility. However, many additional factors likely also drive cross-sectional differences in volatility risk premia. While our paper establishes that volatility is priced in individual equity options, there is still much work to be done to understand how the volatility risk premium varies across stocks and over time.
  Editors: Stefan Nagel, Philip Bond, Amit Seru, and Wei Xiong
